---
title: "Phasey Bean (Macroptilium lathyroides)"
slug: "macroptilium-lathyroides"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354630370/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Phasey Bean"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fabales"
tax_family: "Fabaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fabales", "Fabaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354630370/medium.jpg" alt="Phasey Bean" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/dbond">
dbond</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/200947093">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fabales
-   **Family:** Fabaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=165060)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common in Florida found in disturbed sites, tidal marshes, and coastal areas.

## Natural history notes

A twining or upright legume characterized by its trifoliate leaves and clusters of deep red to purple flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in disturbed areas such as fields, roadsides, and pastures. It grows primarily during the warmer months and, like other legumes, helps enrich the soil by fixing nitrogen.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/165060-Macroptilium-lathyroides)
-   [Local profile](https://plant-directory.ifas.ufl.edu/plant-directory/macroptilium-lathyroides/)
